tasks.title: CSV to JSON Tool
tasks.prompt:
Build a tiny CSV-to-JSON conversion artifact from the locked fixture below. The solution can be a CLI script, browser page, or documented function, but it must include the converted output and a short validation report.

Fixture CSV:
name,email,role
Ada Lovelace,ada@example.test,admin
Grace Hopper,grace@example.test,maintainer
Margaret Hamilton,margaret@example.test,reviewer

expected_output.description: A small conversion tool package with source, fixture, normalized JSON output, and validation notes.
must_include: source file or HTML file for the converter
validation notes covering headers, row count, and empty-field behavior

artifacts.description: How to run the converter and what was validated.
Converted JSON output for the locked fixture.

rubric: Output JSON preserves all rows and field names.
Implementation is small and easy to run.
Validation notes mention edge cases.
No real personal data or secrets are introduced.

allowed_questions: Should emails be treated as real data?

answers.Can I use a library?: Yes, but prefer a small dependency-free implementation if practical.
answers.Should emails be treated as real data?: No. They use example.test and are synthetic fixture data.
answers.Do I need a UI?: No. CLI, single HTML file, or documented function is acceptable.

security_notes: Use only the synthetic fixture data in the prompt.

tasks.title: Task JSON Normalizer

expected_output.description: A valid task.v0 JSON artifact for the messy input.
must_include: artifacts array with markdown and json artifacts

rubric: JSON parses cleanly.
Original intent is preserved.
Evaluation criteria are structured and filterable.
Interviewer constraints are explicit.

allowed_questions: Should output be compact or verbose?
Can I change the task id?

answers.Should I invent missing fields?: Add minimal fields needed for valid task.v0. Keep invented details conservative.

security_notes: Do not include real logs or secrets in the normalized sample.

tasks.title: Markdown Previewer Mini App
tasks.prompt: Build a small markdown previewer mini app. It must show an editor and a preview pane, render headings, links, lists, inline code, and fenced code blocks, and include a short note about how unsafe HTML is handled. Keep it lightweight and reviewable.

expected_output.description: A runnable mini app artifact with source, screenshot, and safety notes.

artifacts.description: Run instructions, feature notes, and safety choice.

rubric: Editor and preview interaction is clear.
Required markdown syntax renders or is explicitly documented.
Unsafe HTML behavior is explained.
UI is polished enough for a short product demo.

allowed_questions: Can I use a markdown library?

answers.Can I use a markdown library?: Yes, if it stays lightweight and the README names the dependency.
answers.Should raw HTML render?: Default to escaping or sanitizing raw HTML and document the choice.

security_notes: Do not render unsanitized user HTML without documenting the risk.

tasks.title: Password Generator Security Mini App
tasks.prompt: Build a small password generator artifact. The user can choose length and character groups, generate a password, copy it, and see a short strength/safety note. Include a security review explaining randomness, defaults, and what is intentionally not stored.

expected_output.description: A mini app plus security review for a password generator.

artifacts.description: Security review covering randomness, storage, and defaults.

rubric: Generator exposes expected controls.
Security review discusses randomness source and storage.
Defaults are conservative.
No generated password is logged into submission notes.

allowed_questions: What default length should I use?

answers.Should I store generated passwords?: No. Do not persist generated passwords.
answers.Can I use Math.random?: Prefer Web Crypto where available and document fallback behavior if any.
answers.What default length should I use?: Use a conservative default of at least 16 characters.

security_notes: Do not include real passwords, credentials, or secret values in artifacts or notes.

tasks.title: Repository Onboarding Triage
tasks.prompt: Inspect the repository root and produce a short onboarding triage artifact for a new engineer. Do not edit source files except the requested artifact.

expected_output.description: A markdown report that identifies the repo purpose, important commands, important directories, and the top risks/blockers for a new contributor.

rubric: Report is specific to the inspected repository.
Commands and paths are real and useful.
Risks are concrete, not generic.
No secrets or private data are copied into the report.

allowed_questions: What files should I inspect first?
What level of detail should the report include?
Can I include commands I did not run?

answers.What files should I inspect first?: Start with README, package/config files, scripts, tests, and top-level directories.
answers.What level of detail should the report i: Short and actionable. Prefer exact paths and commands over broad summaries.
answers.Can I include commands I did not run?: Yes, but mark them as inferred if you did not run them.

security_notes: Do not include environment variables, tokens, private keys, or credential file contents.

tasks.title: Transcript Security Redaction
tasks.prompt:
Given the transcript excerpt below, produce a redacted markdown transcript and a structured JSON security findings file. Do not preserve secret values.

Transcript excerpt:
User: deploy this with token ghp_exampleShouldBeRemoved1234567890
Assistant: I can use AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=AKIAEXAMPLE000000000 and password=hunter2.
Tool: wrote config containing api_key=sk-exampleShouldBeRemoved1234567890

fixture_safety.reason: Synthetic redaction fixture. Values are intentionally fake but scanner-shaped.

expected_output.description: A redacted markdown transcript and JSON findings file with secret classes, severity, and remediation notes.
must_include: findings JSON with at least 3 findings

rubric: No raw secret-like strings remain.
Findings identify GitHub token, AWS key, password, and API key patterns.
Remediation notes are practical.
Output is concise and reviewable.

allowed_questions: Should I keep partial token prefixes?
Should I include the original excerpt?

answers.Should I keep partial token prefixes?: No. Replace secret values fully with [REDACTED].
answers.How detailed should remediation be?: One short actionable remediation per finding.

security_notes: Never copy raw token-like values into output artifacts.
